SymbolicWedderburn","user":{"login":"sebastiendesignolle","id":55443987,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU1NDQzOTg3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/55443987?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle","html_url":"https://github.com/sebastiendesignolle","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/sebastiendesignolle/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-22T13:38:45Z","updated_at":"2024-10-22T21:21:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Given an $m\times m$ matrix $M$, I'm interested in solving relaxations of the problem
```math
\max\left\{\sum_{x,y=1}^mM_{xy}a_xb_y~|~a_1,\ldots,a_m,b_1,\ldots,b_m=\pm1\right\},
```
in particular when the matrix $M$ is highly symmetric.

Here is the way I'm coding it at the moment, but this leads to a lot of unstability in the symmetry reduction, see the attached image.
Am I missing something simple?

```julia
import COSMO
import Dualization
import DynamicPolynomials as DP
import JuMP
import LinearAlgebra as LA
import PermutationGroups as PG # v0.4.3
import Polymake
import SemialgebraicSets
import SumOfSquares as SOS # v0.7.3
# import SymbolicWedderburn # v0.3.7

function test(
    M::Matrix,
    gens::Vector{Vector{Int}} = Vector{Int}[];
    solver = COSMO.Optimizer,
    level = 1
)
    m1, m2 = size(M)
    DP.@polyvar x[1:m1+m2]
    a = x[1:m1]
    b = x[m1+1:m1+m2]
    f = LA.dot(a, M, b)
    S = SemialgebraicSets.intersect([SemialgebraicSets.@set x[i]^2 == 1 for i in 1:m1+m2]...)

    model = SOS.SOSModel(Dualization.dual_optimizer(solver))
    JuMP.@variable(model, t)
    JuMP.@objective(model, Min, t)

    if isempty(gens)
        pattern = nothing
    else
        @assert m1 == m2
        m = m1
        generators = vcat([PG.Perm{UInt16}([g; g .+ m]) for g in gens], [PG.Perm{UInt16}([m+1:2m; 1:m])])
        G = PG.PermGroup(generators)
        pattern = SOS.Symmetry.Pattern(G, SOS.Symmetry.VariablePermutation())
    end

    JuMP.@constraint(model, c, f ≤ t; domain = S, symmetry = pattern, maxdegree = 2level)
    JuMP.set_silent(model)
    JuMP.optimize!(model)
    return JuMP.objective_value(model)
end

# 24-cell as a simple example
pol = Polymake.polytope.wythoff("D4", 1)
# vertices
v = Matrix{Int}(pol.VERTICES)[:, 2:end]
# Gram matrix
p = v * v'
# generators of the action on the vertices, i.e., on the lines/columns of p
gens = Polymake.to_one_based_indexing(pol.GROUP.VERTICES_ACTION.GENERATORS)

# @profview of the following line gives the image below
test(p, gens; level = 1)
```

Also, I'm just starting working with SOS.jl so any comments on my syntax is welcome.
Thanks in advance!

an $m\times m$ matrix $M$, I'm interested in solving relaxations of the problem
```math
\max\left\{\sum_{x,y=1}^mM_{xy}a_xb_y~|~a_1,\ldots,a_m,b_1,\ldots,b_m=\pm1\right\},
```
in particular when the matrix $M$ is highly symmetric.

Here is the way I'm coding it at the moment, but this leads to a lot of unstability in the symmetry reduction, see the attached image.
Am I missing something simple?

```julia
import COSMO
import Dualization
import DynamicPolynomials as DP
import JuMP
import LinearAlgebra as LA
import PermutationGroups as PG # v0.4.3
import Polymake
import SemialgebraicSets
import SumOfSquares as SOS # v0.7.3
# import SymbolicWedderburn # v0.3.7

function test(
    M::Matrix,
    gens::Vector{Vector{Int}} = Vector{Int}[];
    solver = COSMO.Optimizer,
    level = 1
)
    m1, m2 = size(M)
    DP.@polyvar x[1:m1+m2]
    a = x[1:m1]
    b = x[m1+1:m1+m2]
    f = LA.dot(a, M, b)
    S = SemialgebraicSets.intersect([SemialgebraicSets.@set x[i]^2 == 1 for i in 1:m1+m2]...)

    model = SOS.SOSModel(Dualization.dual_optimizer(solver))
    JuMP.@variable(model, t)
    JuMP.@objective(model, Min, t)

    if isempty(gens)
        pattern = nothing
    else
        @assert m1 == m2
        m = m1
        generators = vcat([PG.Perm{UInt16}([g; g .+ m]) for g in gens], [PG.Perm{UInt16}([m+1:2m; 1:m])])
        G = PG.PermGroup(generators)
        pattern = SOS.Symmetry.Pattern(G, SOS.Symmetry.VariablePermutation())
    end

    JuMP.@constraint(model, c, f ≤ t; domain = S, symmetry = pattern, maxdegree = 2level)
    JuMP.set_silent(model)
    JuMP.optimize!(model)
    return JuMP.objective_value(model)
end

# 24-cell as a simple example
pol = Polymake.polytope.wythoff("D4", 1)
# vertices
v = Matrix{Int}(pol.VERTICES)[:, 2:end]
# Gram matrix
p = v * v'
# generators of the action on the vertices, i.e., on the lines/columns of p
gens = Polymake.to_one_based_indexing(pol.GROUP.VERTICES_ACTION.GENERATORS)

# @profview of the following line gives the image below
test(p, gens; level = 1)
```

Also, I'm just starting working with SOS.jl so any comments on my syntax is welcome.
Thanks in advance!

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

I think what happens is that SCS is precompiled before MKL_jll is fully installed (it's lazy artifact), so the precompilation fails as even though SCS_MKL_jll is loaded the library (`libscsmkl`) cannot be dlopened.

I'm not sure what we could do about it either, I just wanted to leave this as a permanent in case somebody else searches for this. https://github.com/kalmarek/KnuthBendix.jl/actions/runs/10182873828/job/28166477367?pr=75

This happens on julia-1.10.4, but NOT on julia-1.6.7 or nightly.

As you can see the lastest commit:
https://github.com/kalmarek/KnuthBendix.jl/pull/75/commits/64691e6138dadff0fe345ef7041c8ea9850c8ec1
defines `Stack{W}` as an alias and replaces every `Vector{Tuple{W,W}}` with `Stack{W}`

I confirmed locally that the tests on the previous commit 2e194aa14d6b604ceb9762aadb76b96b4d35fc9a
run fine locally on julia-1.10.4

Any hint why this might happen?

was also thinking that we have two different approaches here to accomplish something very similar:
1. You're using polynomials (free objects) and quotient basis (reducing them modulo an ideal).
2. I use words over an alphabet (free objects) to represent group elements (e.g. as words rewritten to their ~canonical forms) which then end up in the Dirac basis (generic w.r.t. the type of elements)

Your approach seems a bit more "lazy" but I have some doubts if this could be applied to my case as I want to eagerly reduce the number of words as soon as possible to avoid duplication.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SumOfSquares.jl/issues/comments/2143365271/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I've been waiting for using this already quite some time but it's really hard without any docs ;) e.g. can a non-isbits `x` be in a non-consistent state when read from `at_mem::AtomicMemory` (e.g. some other thread was modifying it at the same time)?
I presume that the whole point of `AtomicMemory` is that one does not need to use `@atomic`, but I'd be nice to have it stated in clear text here.

also a tangent question, but the next building block would be a CAS operation on an `AtomicMemory`, but neither this docstring nor `man-atomic-operations` mention it (except for `Threads.atomic_cas!`      AtomicMemory{T} == GenericMemory{:atomic, T, Core.CPU}
 
-One-dimensional dense array with elements of type `T`, where each element is
+One-dimensional, fixed-size, dense array with elements of type `T`, where each element is
 independently atomic when accessed, and cannot be set non-atomically.

Reads from `AtomicMemory` default to atomic reads with `:monotonic` ordering.
Writes to `AtomicMemory` require specifying explicit ordering via the [`@atomic`](@ref atomic) macro.

!!! note
    Currently the interface for `AtomicMemory` has not stabilized yet and is therefore subject to change without notice.

For details, see [Atomic Operations](@ref man-atomic-operations)

The Manifesto was modified last time one year ago and doesn't even mention `AtomicMemory` so I was hesitant to rely on the document.

What I understand is that the plan is to implement
* `@atomic ord x[3]` → `Core.memoryrefget(memoryref(x, 3), ord, ...)`
* `@atomic ord x[3] = 4` → `Core.memoryrefset!(memoryref(x, 3), 4, ord, ...)`
* `@atomicswap ord x[3]` → `Core.memoryrefswap!(memoryref(x, 3), 4, ord, ...)`
* `@atomicreplace ord x[3] 3=>4` → `Core.memoryrefreplace!(memoryref(x, 3), 3, 4, ord, ...)`

to extend the `@atomic[...]` macros? (So far these work only on atomic fields). I'd happily give it a try myself if that's the goal +!!! warning","path":"base/genericmemory.jl","commit_id":"05593830d523e9a973ac5076573a4224c23fb66c","original_commit_id":"05593830d523e9a973ac5076573a4224c23fb66c","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I'm not sure if "each element is independently atomic when accessed, and cannot be set non-atomically." is too vague. The precise meaning is that 
* `x = mem[i]` will be performed atomically (with `:monotonic` ordering)
* to store `x` somewhere in `mem` the use of `@atomic` (+ specifying ordering?) macro is necessary, i.e. `@atomic :sequentially_consistent mem[i] = x` is the only legal way to do so (without dropping to `Core.memoryref[...]` buildins).  

How about 
```
Fixed-size [`DenseVector{T}`](@ref DenseVector). Access to its any of its elements is performed atomically (with `:monotonic` ordering). Moreover setting any of the elements must be accomplished using the `@atomic` macro and explicitly specifying ordering.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1631531999","pull_request_review_id":2105102313,"id":1631531999,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5hPzPf","diff_hunk":"@@ -423,6 +423,69 @@ let a = ARefxy(1, -1)
     @test_throws ConcurrencyViolationError @atomicreplace :monotonic :acquire a.x xchg
 end
 
+let a = AtomicMemory{Float64}(undef, 2)","path":"test/atomics.jl","commit_id":"6980cd32b04def2fb407cb8597e00deeaa163e65","original_commit_id":"c7f721c8ffc473fa4aab30873b84e68448a556a5","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"sure, I can add more tests with e.g. `APadInt` and maybe an index different from 1 ;) I'm not sure though how much more tests are needed.
I see that the primitives (`memoryref*`) are very well tested and `@atomic` macros do nothing more than using them as intended. Adding lots of tests on the higher level doesn't seem to add much value.

I'd suggest a very similar testset for 
1. `AtomicMemory{APadInt}` (non-standard size)
2. `AtomicMemory{Union{Nothing, Int}}` (union type) and
3. `AtomicMemory{Vector{BigInt}}` (non-isbitstype, contains pointer)  

----

What I'd like to test more is the code generated by `@atomic` macro, but I didn't find a straightforward way of comparing e.g. 
```julia
ex = @macroexpand @atomic ...
@test ex == test_ex 
```
without building the `test_ex` by hand. Any tips on this?

As for "real" examples I will soon™ have something, but it might be a bit too involved to be included in the tests here...      AtomicMemory{T} == GenericMemory{:atomic, T, Core.CPU}
 
 Fixed-size [`DenseVector{T}`](@ref DenseVector).
-Access to its any of its elements is performed atomically (with `:monotonic` ordering).
-Setting any of the elements must be accomplished using the `@atomic` macro and explicitly specifying ordering.
+Fetching of any of its individual elements is performed atomically
+(with `:monotonic` ordering by default).
 
 !!! warning
-    Each element is independently atomic when accessed, and cannot be set non-atomically.
-    Currently the `@atomic` macro and higher level interface have not been completed,
-    but the building blocks for a future implementation are the internal intrinsics
-    `Core.memoryrefget`, `Core.memoryrefset!`, `Core.memoryref_isassigned`, `Core.memoryrefswap!`,
-    `Core.memoryrefmodify!`, and `Core.memoryrefreplace!`.
+    The access to `AtomicMemory` must be done by either using the [`@atomic`](@ref)
+    macro or the lower level interface functions: `Base.getindex_atomic`,
+    `Base.setindex_atomic!`, `Base.setindexonce_atomic!`,
+    `Base.swapindex!`, `Base.modifyindex!`, and `Base.replaceindex!`.","path":"base/genericmemory.jl","commit_id":"f4e6ca25c46628e43468c47ad1e0bf4e1d1fd403","original_commit_id":"f4e6ca25c46628e43468c47ad1e0bf4e1d1fd403","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"`getindex` has already implementation for `GenericMemory` and with the order of arguments it was impossible to distinguish them. Here is a minimal example, which is similar to an example in the SumsOfSquares document. Here, I used ```poly = sum(x) + sum(x.^4)``` instead of ```poly = sum(x) + sum(x.^2)```


````julia
import MutableArithmetics as MA
using MultivariatePolynomials
using MultivariateBases
using DynamicPolynomials
@polyvar x[1:3]
poly = sum(x) + sum(x.^4)
using PermutationGroups
G = PermGroup([perm"(1,2,3)",perm"(1,2)"])
import CSDP
solver = CSDP.Optimizer
model = Model(solver)
@variable(model, t)
@objective(model, Max, t)
pattern = Symmetry.Pattern(G, Symmetry.VariablePermutation())
con_ref = @constraint model poly - t in SOSCone() symmetry = pattern
optimize!(model)
value(t)
```````````

@kalmarek has checked the problem and said "Indeed this seems to be the problem with [SumOfSquares.jl](https://juliahub.com/ui/Packages/General/SumOfSquares) formulation, as SymbolicWedderburn.jl correctly reduces the optimization problem"

For more info:

Do you want me to replace e.g. the existing `getindex_atomic` by the following?
```julia
function getindex_atomic(mem::GenericMemory, order::Symbol, i::Union{Integer,CartesianIndex}...)
    return getindex_atomic(mem, order, CartesianIndex(to_indices(mem, i)))
end
function getindex_atomic(mem::GenericMemory, order::Symbol, i::CartesianIndex)
    return getindex_atomic(mem, order, Tuple(i)...)
end
function getindex_atomic(mem::GenericMemory, order::Symbol, i::Integer...)
    idcs = to_indices(mem, i)
    S = IndexStyle(mem)
    if isa(S, IndexLinear)
        return getindex_atomic(mem, order, _to_linear_index(mem, idcs...))
    else
        return getindex_atomic(mem, order, _to_subscript_indices(mem, idcs...)...)
    end
end
function getindex_atomic(mem::GenericMemory, order::Symbol, i::Int)
    memref = memoryref(mem, i)
    return memoryrefget(memref, order, @_boundscheck)
end
```
or something yet different? Since `IndexStyle of `GenericMemory` is defined to be `IndexLinear` by abstract method, the other branch will be empty until someone actually implements some funky `GenericMemory`.

Similar methods will need to be added for `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!`, `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, and `replaceindex_atomic!`.

Is that what you      AtomicMemory{T} == GenericMemory{:atomic, T, Core.CPU}
 
 Fixed-size [`DenseVector{T}`](@ref DenseVector).
-Access to its any of its elements is performed atomically (with `:monotonic` ordering).
-Setting any of the elements must be accomplished using the `@atomic` macro and explicitly specifying ordering.
+Fetching of any of its individual elements is performed atomically
+(with `:monotonic` ordering by default).
 
 !!! warning
-    Each element is independently atomic when accessed, and cannot be set non-atomically.
-    Currently the `@atomic` macro and higher level interface have not been completed,
-    but the building blocks for a future implementation are the internal intrinsics
-    `Core.memoryrefget`, `Core.memoryrefset!`, `Core.memoryref_isassigned`, `Core.memoryrefswap!`,
-    `Core.memoryrefmodify!`, and `Core.memoryrefreplace!`.
+    The access to `AtomicMemory` must be done by either using the [`@atomic`](@ref)
+    macro or the lower level interface functions: `Base.getindex_atomic`,
+    `Base.setindex_atomic!`, `Base.setindexonce_atomic!`,
+    `Base.swapindex!`, `Base.modifyindex!`, and the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

```julia
## vararg methods generation
for (fn, args) in (
    :(getindex_atomic(mem::GenericMemory, order::Symbol, i::Int)),
    :(setindex_atomic!(mem::GenericMemory, order::Symbol, val, i::Int)),
    :(setindexonce_atomic!(mem::GenericMemory, success_order::Symbol, fail_order::Symbol, val, i::Int)),
    :(modifyindex_atomic!(mem::GenericMemory, order::Symbol, op, val, i::Int)),
    :(swapindex_atomic!(mem::GenericMemory, order::Symbol, val, i::Int)),
    :(replaceindex_atomic!(mem::GenericMemory, success_order::Symbol, fail_order::Symbol, expected, desired, i::Int,)),
)
    fn = ex.args[1]
    args = ex.args[2:end-1]

    @eval begin
        function $fn($(args...), i::Union{Integer,CartesianIndex}...)
            return $fn($(args...), CartesianIndex(to_indices($(args[1]), i)))
        end

        function $fn($(args...), i::CartesianIndex)
            return $fn($(args...), Tuple(i)...)
        end

        function $fn($(args...), i::Integer...)
            idcs = to_indices($(args[1]), i)
            S = IndexStyle($(args[1]))
            if isa(S, IndexLinear)
                return $fn($(args...), _to_linear_index($(args[1]), idcs...))
            else
                return $fn($(args...), _to_subscript_indices($(args[1]), idcs...))
            end
        end
    end
end
```
which works just fine (as julia code), but doesn't pass bootstrap. the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

the discussion in #54642

Implemented:
- [x] `modifyindex_atomic!`, `swapindex_atomic!`, `replaceindex_atomic!` for `GenericMemory`
- [x] `getindex_atomic`, `setindex_atomic!`, `setindexonce_atomic!` for `GenericMemory` 
- [x] add support for references in `@atomic` macros
- [x] add support for vararg indices in `@atomic` macros 
- [x] tests
- [x] update docstrings with example usage
- [ ] update Atomics section of the manual (?)
- [x] news

@oscardssmith @vtjnash 

# New `@atomic` transformations implemented here:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, :sequentially_consistent, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2])
:(Base.getindex_atomic(a, order, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicswap order a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.swapindex_atomic!(a, order, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomic order a[i1,i2] += 2.0)
:((Base.modifyindex_atomic!(a, order, +, 2.0, i1, i2))[2])

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomiconce o1 o2 a[i1,i2] = 2.0)
:(Base.setindexonce_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, :sequentially_consistent, :sequentially_consistent, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

julia> @macroexpand (@atomicreplace o1 o2 a[i1,i2] (2.0=>3.0))
:(Base.replaceindex_atomic!(a, o1, o2, 2.0, 3.0, i1, i2))

README says
> The `MA.operate!!(op::Function, x, args...)` redirects to `op(x, args...)` if `x` is not mutable or if the result of the operation cannot be stored in `x`. Otherwise, it redirects to `MA.operate!(op, x, args...)`.

but actually it redirects only to `MA.operate(op, x, args...)`.

As for the future I'd be great if a tutorial page on implementing e.g. MA for Rationals is provided

out `broadcast!` is well implemented for SparseArrays:
```julia
julia> v = sparsevec([1, 3], [π, 2π])
3-element SparseVector{Float64, Int64} with 2 stored entries:
  [1]  =  3.14159
  [3]  =  6.28319

julia> a = copy(v)
3-element SparseVector{Float64, Int64} with 2 stored entries:
  [1]  =  3.14159
  [3]  =  6.28319

julia> b = copy(v)
3-element SparseVector{Float64, Int64} with 2 stored entries:
  [1]  =  3.14159
  [3]  =  6.28319

julia> @btime Broadcast.broadcast!(+, $v, 3, $a, 2, $b)
  38.791 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
3-element SparseVector{Float64, Int64} with 3 stored entries:
  [1]  =  11.2832
  [2]  =  5.0
  [3]  =  17.5664

julia> @btime Broadcast.broadcast!(-, $v, 3, $a)
  27.560 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
3-element SparseVector{Float64, Int64} with 3 stored entries:
  [1]  =  -0.141593
  [2]  =  3.0
  [3]  =  -3.28319

julia> @btime Broadcast.broadcast!(-, $v, $a, $b)
  31.149 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
3-element SparseVector{Float64, Int64} with 0 stored entries
```
Since what we need exactly matches the definition of `broadcast!`, we might as well call `broadcast!` by default for any `AbstractArray` which is what this PR After thinking maybe what Mose suggested is a better option:
* make SCS_MKL_jll depends on MKL_jll unconditionally
* use Require based on the presence of `SCS_MKL_jll` rather than `MKL_jll`

Then users workflow would look like
```julia
Pkg.add("SCS")
Pkg.add("SCS_MKL_jll")
import SCS_MKL_jll
using SCS
```

This way it's the role of SCS_MKL_jll to take care of its deps, not of the user. What do you think?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/comments/1586273414/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/events","html_url":"https://github.com/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266","id":1657466712,"node_id":"I_kwDOAS9gUc5iyu9Y","number":266,"title":"very slow transfer from JuMP to SCS?","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-04-06T13:50:10Z","updated_at":"2023-04-06T13:50:10Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"COLLABORATOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm actually not sure if this is the correct place to report this, but here it is:

My problem has a single psd constraint of size 7_381 with 22_043_332 linear constraints (veeeery sparse ones). After preprocessing (i.e. symmetry reduction:) I get 10 psd constraints of sizes
```
[334, 332, 182, 167, 165, 153, 459, 447, 449, 439]
```
and `459_849` (somewhat denser, but still on average `1e-4` to `1e-5` density) constraints.

The latter problem I can create easily in JuMP (it takes about ~75 minutes). However calling `optimize!` with `SCS.Optimizer` attached results in
* long waiting times (>36h),
* very slowly growing memory consumption (from ~60GB to 128GB, until julia process is killed)
* uninterruptable julia process (sending SIGINT has no effect so I was not able for now to inspect what is really happening).

However I believe all of this happens before the problem is handled to `libscs` (e.g. I get no printout of SCS header).

Are these reasonable sizes for JuMP/MOI/bridges (? I'm shooting in the dark here) to behave well? Where should I start debugging?

@odow, @blegat","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/events","html_url":"https://github.com/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266","id":1657466712,"node_id":"I_kwDOAS9gUc5iyu9Y","number":266,"title":"very slow transfer from JuMP to SCS?","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-04-06T13:50:10Z","updated_at":"2023-04-06T15:48:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"COLLABORATOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm actually not sure if this is the correct place to report this, but here it is:

My problem has a single psd constraint of size 7_381 with 22_043_332 linear constraints (veeeery sparse ones). After preprocessing (i.e. symmetry reduction:) I get 10 psd constraints of sizes
```
[334, 332, 182, 167, 165, 153, 459, 447, 449, 439]
```
and `459_849` (somewhat denser, but still on average `1e-4` to `1e-5` density) constraints.

The latter problem I can create easily in JuMP (it takes about ~75 minutes). However calling `optimize!` with `SCS.Optimizer` attached results in
* long waiting times (>36h),
* very slowly growing memory consumption (from ~60GB to 128GB, until julia process is killed)
* uninterruptable julia process (sending SIGINT has no effect so I was not able for now to inspect what is really happening).

However I believe all of this happens before the problem is handled to `libscs` (e.g. I get no printout of SCS header).

Are these reasonable sizes for JuMP/MOI/bridges (? I'm shooting in the dark here) to behave well? Where should I start debugging?

My problem has a single psd constraint of size 7_381 with 22_043_332 linear constraints (veeeery sparse ones). After preprocessing (i.e. symmetry reduction:) I get 10 psd constraints of sizes
```
[334, 332, 182, 167, 165, 153, 459, 447, 449, 439]
```
and `459_849` (somewhat denser, but still on average `1e-4` to `1e-5` density) constraints.

The latter problem I can create easily in JuMP (it takes about ~75 minutes). However calling `optimize!` with `SCS.Optimizer` attached results in
* long waiting times (>36h),
* very slowly growing memory consumption (from ~60GB to 128GB, until julia process is killed)
* uninterruptable julia process (sending SIGINT has no effect so I was not able for now to inspect what is really happening).

However I believe all of this happens before the problem is handled to `libscs` (e.g. I get no printout of SCS header).

Are these reasonable sizes for JuMP/MOI/bridges (? I'm shooting in the dark here) to behave well? Where should I start debugging?

My problem has a single psd constraint of size 7_381 with 22_043_332 linear constraints (veeeery sparse ones). After preprocessing (i.e. symmetry reduction:) I get 10 psd constraints of sizes
```
[334, 332, 182, 167, 165, 153, 459, 447, 449, 439]
```
and `459_849` (somewhat denser, but still on average `1e-4` to `1e-5` density) constraints.

The latter problem I can create easily in JuMP (it takes about ~75 minutes). However calling `optimize!` with `SCS.Optimizer` attached results in
* long waiting times (>36h),
* very slowly growing memory consumption (from ~60GB to 128GB, until julia process is killed)
* uninterruptable julia process (sending SIGINT has no effect so I was not able for now to inspect what is really happening).

However I believe all of this happens before the problem is handled to `libscs` (e.g. I get no printout of SCS header).

Are these reasonable sizes for JuMP/MOI/bridges (? I'm shooting in the dark here) to behave well? Where should I start debugging?

@odow, @blegat","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/comments/1500541447","html_url":"https://github.com/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266#issuecomment-1500541447","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/266","id":1500541447,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAS9gUc5ZcHIH","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-04-07T18:44:53Z","updated_at":"2023-04-07T18:44:53Z","author_association":"COLLABORATOR","body":"well, it will take scs some time to finish :)

My problem has a single psd constraint of size 7_381 with 22_043_332 linear constraints (veeeery sparse ones). After preprocessing (i.e. symmetry reduction:) I get 10 psd constraints of sizes
```
[334, 332, 182, 167, 165, 153, 459, 447, 449, 439]
```
and `459_849` (somewhat denser, but still on average `1e-4` to `1e-5` density) constraints.

The latter problem I can create easily in JuMP (it takes about ~75 minutes). However calling `optimize!` with `SCS.Optimizer` attached results in
* long waiting times (>36h),
* very slowly growing memory consumption (from ~60GB to 128GB, until julia process is killed)
* uninterruptable julia process (sending SIGINT has no effect so I was not able for now to inspect what is really happening).

However I believe all of this happens before the problem is handled to `libscs` (e.g. I get no printout of SCS header).

Are these reasonable sizes for JuMP/MOI/bridges (? I'm shooting in the dark here) to behave well? Where should I start debugging?

Sorry for the confusion. The problem SCS sees looks is

```
min t
s.t t ≥ -1.0
    [ ... ] # # same constraints as above
```

Yes, I know that my problem will have a (trivial) solution when `t=0`; I'm interested in solutions with `t << 0` (and sufficiently large, so that I can certify the solution).

So sometimes I start with `t ≥ -0.1` and then get warmstart, decrement the lower bound in by 0.05 and solve again. when SCS can't get the optimal solution quickly this method works reasonably well.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/events","html_url":"https://github.com/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269","id":1667152601,"node_id":"I_kwDOAS9gUc5jXrrZ","number":269,"title":"Do we need Requires?","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-04-13T21:24:55Z","updated_at":"2023-04-13T21:24:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"COLLABORATOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I just did an experiment locally and it turns out we could just drop `Requires` and remove the whole `__init__()` hackery:

```julia
# Copyright (c) 2014: SCS.jl contributors
#
# Use of this source code is governed by an MIT-style license that can be found
# in the LICENSE.md file or at https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT.

module SCS

import MathOptInterface
import Requires
import SCS_jll
import SparseArrays

global indirect = SCS_jll.libscsindir
global direct = SCS_jll.libscsdir

import SCS_GPU_jll
global gpuindirect = SCS_GPU_jll.libscsgpuindir

if Sys.islinux() && Sys.ARCH == :x86_64
    import SCS_MKL_jll
    import SCS_MKL_jll.MKL_jll
    global mkldirect = SCS_MKL_jll.libscsmkl
end

include("c_wrapper.jl")
include("linear_solvers/direct.jl")
include("linear_solvers/indirect.jl")
include("linear_solvers/gpu_indirect.jl")
include("linear_solvers/mkl_direct.jl")
include("MOI_wrapper/MOI_wrapper.jl")

export scs_solve

end
```

works just fine. The only difference is that when you try to use e.g. `SCS.GpuIndirectSolver` without installing CUDA first you'll get (sorry for errors in german ;)
```julia
julia> feasible_basic_problems(SCS.GpuIndirectSolver)
ERROR: could not load library "/home/kalmar/.julia/artifacts/2aaf2d3c367d9e1a41ebf36b0bf47a08f363b09b/lib/libscsgpuindir.so"
libcudart.so.11.0: Kann die Shared-Object-Datei nicht öffnen: Datei oder Verzeichnis nicht gefunden
Stacktrace:
 [1] scs_set_default_settings
   @ ~/.julia/dev/SCS/src/linear_solvers/gpu_indirect.jl:14 [inlined]
 [2] ScsSettings
   @ ~/.julia/dev/SCS/src/c_wrapper.jl:43 [inlined]
 [3] scs_solve(linear_solver::Type{SCS.GpuIndirectSolver}, m::Int64, n::Int64, A::Matrix{Float64}, P::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int64}, b::Vector{Float64}, c::Vector{Float64}, z::Int64, l::Int64, bu::Vector{Float64}, bl::Vector{Float64}, q::Vector{Int64}, s::Vector{Int64}, ep::Int64, ed::Int64, p::Vector{Float64}, primal_sol::Vector{Float64}, dual_sol::Vector{Float64}, slack::Vector{Float64}; warm_start::Bool, options::Base.Pairs{Symbol, Union{}, Tuple{}, NamedTuple{(), Tuple{}}})
   @ SCS ~/.julia/dev/SCS/src/c_wrapper.jl:319
 [4] scs_solve (repeats 2 times)
   @ ~/.julia/dev/SCS/src/c_wrapper.jl:278 [inlined]
 [5] feasible_basic_problems(solver::Type)
   @ Main ~/.julia/dev/SCS/test/test_problems.jl:701
 [6] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```
If CUDA (e.g. through `CUDA_jll`) is installed but no GPU is present SCS will throw a standard CUDA error (`no CUDA device found` or so).

----

The benefits of doing this:
 * it's enough to install `MKL_jll`/`CUDA_jll` somewhere and `MKLDirectSolver`/`GpuIndirectSolver` will be operational
 * no need to load `MKL_jll`/`CUDA_jll` before `SCS`,
 * `CUDA_jll` is deprecated (and it has just a dozen new users in the last month, see https://juliahub.com/ui/Packages/CUDA_jll/nSfHz/11.8.0+0), so we will need to move away from it anyway; `CUDA_Runtime_jll` (8.5k new users) is the new way to go, although I haven't figured out how to use it yet.

Drawbacks of dropping `Requires`
 * we no longer have fine-grained control over CUDA/MKL versions loaded, or even we don't know if the jll version is used (=potentially more user-facing bugs which are hard to diagnose)
 * `available_solvers` are gone (but one could cook up some `__init__` to bring them back).

@odow Are we happy with the status quo, should we load SCS_GPU_jll unconditionally, or should we work on a solution with `CUDA_Runtime_jll`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/events","html_url":"https://github.com/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269","id":1667152601,"node_id":"I_kwDOAS9gUc5jXrrZ","number":269,"title":"Do we need Requires?","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-04-13T21:24:55Z","updated_at":"2023-04-13T21:57:12Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"COLLABORATOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I just did an experiment locally and it turns out we could just drop `Requires` and remove the whole `__init__()` hackery:

```julia
# Copyright (c) 2014: SCS.jl contributors
#
# Use of this source code is governed by an MIT-style license that can be found
# in the LICENSE.md file or at https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT.

module SCS

import MathOptInterface
import Requires
import SCS_jll
import SparseArrays

global indirect = SCS_jll.libscsindir
global direct = SCS_jll.libscsdir

import SCS_GPU_jll
global gpuindirect = SCS_GPU_jll.libscsgpuindir

if Sys.islinux() && Sys.ARCH == :x86_64
    import SCS_MKL_jll
    import SCS_MKL_jll.MKL_jll
    global mkldirect = SCS_MKL_jll.libscsmkl
end

include("c_wrapper.jl")
include("linear_solvers/direct.jl")
include("linear_solvers/indirect.jl")
include("linear_solvers/gpu_indirect.jl")
include("linear_solvers/mkl_direct.jl")
include("MOI_wrapper/MOI_wrapper.jl")

export scs_solve

end
```

works just fine. The only difference is that when you try to use e.g. `SCS.GpuIndirectSolver` without installing CUDA first you'll get (sorry for errors in german ;)
```julia
julia> feasible_basic_problems(SCS.GpuIndirectSolver)
ERROR: could not load library "/home/kalmar/.julia/artifacts/2aaf2d3c367d9e1a41ebf36b0bf47a08f363b09b/lib/libscsgpuindir.so"
libcudart.so.11.0: Kann die Shared-Object-Datei nicht öffnen: Datei oder Verzeichnis nicht gefunden
Stacktrace:
 [1] scs_set_default_settings
   @ ~/.julia/dev/SCS/src/linear_solvers/gpu_indirect.jl:14 [inlined]
 [2] ScsSettings
   @ ~/.julia/dev/SCS/src/c_wrapper.jl:43 [inlined]
 [3] scs_solve(linear_solver::Type{SCS.GpuIndirectSolver}, m::Int64, n::Int64, A::Matrix{Float64}, P::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int64}, b::Vector{Float64}, c::Vector{Float64}, z::Int64, l::Int64, bu::Vector{Float64}, bl::Vector{Float64}, q::Vector{Int64}, s::Vector{Int64}, ep::Int64, ed::Int64, p::Vector{Float64}, primal_sol::Vector{Float64}, dual_sol::Vector{Float64}, slack::Vector{Float64}; warm_start::Bool, options::Base.Pairs{Symbol, Union{}, Tuple{}, NamedTuple{(), Tuple{}}})
   @ SCS ~/.julia/dev/SCS/src/c_wrapper.jl:319
 [4] scs_solve (repeats 2 times)
   @ ~/.julia/dev/SCS/src/c_wrapper.jl:278 [inlined]
 [5] feasible_basic_problems(solver::Type)
   @ Main ~/.julia/dev/SCS/test/test_problems.jl:701
 [6] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```
If CUDA (e.g. through `CUDA_jll`) is installed but no GPU is present SCS will throw a standard CUDA error (`no CUDA device found` or so).

----

The benefits of doing this:
 * it's enough to install `MKL_jll`/`CUDA_jll` somewhere and `MKLDirectSolver`/`GpuIndirectSolver` will be operational
 * no need to load `MKL_jll`/`CUDA_jll` before `SCS`,
 * `CUDA_jll` is deprecated (and it has just a dozen new users in the last month, see https://juliahub.com/ui/Packages/CUDA_jll/nSfHz/11.8.0+0), so we will need to move away from it anyway; `CUDA_Runtime_jll` (8.5k new users) is the new way to go, although I haven't figured out how to use it yet.

Drawbacks of dropping `Requires`
 * we no longer have fine-grained control over CUDA/MKL versions loaded, or even we don't know if the jll version is used (=potentially more user-facing bugs which are hard to diagnose)
 * `available_solvers` are gone (but one could cook up some `__init__` to bring them back).

 * we no longer have fine-grained control over CUDA/MKL versions loaded, or even we don't know if the jll version is used (=potentially more user-facing bugs which are hard to diagnose)
 * `available_solvers` are gone (but one could cook up some `__init__` to bring them back).

@odow Are we happy with the status quo, should we load SCS_GPU_jll unconditionally, or should we work on a solution with `CUDA_Runtime_jll`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jump-dev/SCS.jl/issues/269/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

@LilithHafner I just built it again and get these results:

```julia
julia> using Random; Random.seed!(1)
TaskLocalRNG()

julia> x = rand(-3:3, 5,5).//rand(1:6, 5, 5)
5×5 Matrix{Rational{Int64}}:
 -1//2  -2//3    0     1//4    0
 -1//3   2//3    0    -2//3    1
  1//6   2//5  -1//3  -3//5  -1//3
  1//6   1//4   -3    -1//3    1
  3//5  -2//3    0     -1      0


julia> x[:, 1] .= 0;

julia> x
5×5 Matrix{Rational{Int64}}:
 0  -2//3    0     1//4    0
 0   2//3    0    -2//3    1
 0   2//5  -1//3  -3//5  -1//3
 0   1//4   -3    -1//3    1
 0  -2//3    0     -1      0

julia> x[:, 2] .= -1;

julia> x
5×5 Matrix{Rational{Int64}}:
 0  -1    0     1//4    0
 0  -1    0    -2//3    1
 0  -1  -1//3  -3//5  -1//3
 0  -1   -3    -1//3    1
 0  -1    0     -1      0

julia> x[:, end] = rand(-4:4, 5);

julia> x
5×5 Matrix{Rational{Int64}}:
 0  -1    0     1//4   2
 0  -1    0    -2//3  -4
 0  -1  -1//3  -3//5   0
 0  -1   -3    -1//3  -3
 0  -1    0     -1     0

```
To me it seems that everything runs as intended;
Should I add more tests? {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870","id":1490290679,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Y1Af3","number":47870,"title":"putting getindex(::NTuple, ::NTuple) into standard library","user":{"login":"mchristianl","id":28118027,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4MTE4MDI3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28118027?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl","html_url":"https://github.com/mchristianl","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mchristianl/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-12-11T18:08:34Z","updated_at":"2022-12-11T18:43:47Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi,

It might be a bit of a question of taste, but ...
Would it be possible to add a `getindex` method to the standard library such that tuples can be indexed with tuples?

E.g.

```julia
t = (10,20,30,40)
σ = (2,4)
t[σ] # MethodError: no method matching getindex(::NTuple{4, Int64}, ::Tuple{Int64, Int64}) ↯
ntuple(i -> t[σ[i]],length(σ)) :: NTuple # (20,40) works as expected ✓
```

This kind of operation seems to occur a lot when computing with **permutations** (and _combinations_): e.g. having a tuple of the 4 corner points of a tetrahedron and then iterating over all its edges. This can be done in such a way

```julia
# compute the total length of all edges of a tetrahedron
@noinline function edge_length(Ps::NTuple{4,NTuple{3}})
  l = 0.0
  @inline norm(v) = sqrt(max(0,sum(v .* v))) # allow inlining
  σs = ((1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (2, 3), (2, 4), (3, 4)) # some permutation
  for σ in σs
    Qs = ntuple(i -> Ps[σ[i]],length(σ)) # Qs = Ps[σ]
    l += norm(.-(Qs...)) # do something with Qs
  end
  return l
end

Ps = ((0,0,0),(0,0,1),(0,1,0),(1,0,0))
@code_native debuginfo=:none edge_length(Ps)
using BenchmarkTools
@benchmark edge_length($Ps) # ~50 cycles
```

This might also bother other people, because non-allocating idioms for `Ps[σ]` are
- `ntuple(i -> Ps[σ[i]],length(σ))`
- `Tuple(SVector(Ps)[SVector(σ)])`

so one might use SVector all the time, but these need an `SVector(...)` constructor and several conversions when dealing with a datastructure that uses tuples.

Could there be any reason against this?

kind regards","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1345627772","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870#issuecomment-1345627772","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47870","id":1345627772,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5QNKZ8","user":{"login":"kalmarek","id":1666118,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2NjYxMTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1666118?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek","html_url":"https://github.com/kalmarek","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kalmarek/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-12-11T18:43:47Z","updated_at":"2022-12-11T18:43:47Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"maybe it's a bit orthogonal to the issue of adding the syntax but here you're treating a tuple as permutation. If you stop doing it, namely write:
```julia
struct Perm{N,T<:Integer}
    cycle::NTuple{N,T}
end
Base.getindex(t::Tuple, p::Perm{N}) where N = ntuple(i->t[p.cycle[i]], N)

@noinline function edge_length2(Ps::NTuple{4,NTuple{3}}, σs)
  l = 0.0
  @inline norm(v) = sqrt(max(0,sum(v .* v))) # allow inlining
  for σ in σs
    Qs = Ps[σ]
    l += norm(.-(Qs...)) # do something with Qs
  end
  return l
end

σs2 = Perm{2,Int}.(((1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (2, 3), (2, 4), (3, 4))) # some permutation
@btime edge_length2($Ps, $σs2)
```
the result is the same and equally fast (at least on my computer: ~12-14 ns)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1345627772/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

On a small problem I get
```
problem:  variables n: 2640, constraints m: 5499
cones:    z: primal zero / dual free vars: 2860
          s: psd vars: 2639, ssize: 10
settings: eps_abs: 1.0e-10, eps_rel: 1.0e-10, eps_infeas: 1.0e-07
          alpha: 1.90, scale: 1.00e-01, adaptive_scale: 1
          max_iters: 20000, normalize: 1, rho_x: 1.00e-06
          acceleration_lookback: 50, acceleration_interval: 10
lin-sys:  sparse-direct-amd-qdldl
          nnz(A): 66208, nnz(P): 0
```
 * `43.913933 seconds (179.25 k allocations: 15.281 MiB)` vs
 * `20.055237 seconds (179.25 k allocations: 15.281 MiB)`
i.e. a **2.19**-speed up;

On a larger one
```
problem:  variables n: 5708, constraints m: 11938
cones:    z: primal zero / dual free vars: 6231
          s: psd vars: 5707, ssize: 20
settings: eps_abs: 1.0e-10, eps_rel: 1.0e-10, eps_infeas: 1.0e-07
          alpha: 1.90, scale: 1.00e-01, adaptive_scale: 1
          max_iters: 20000, normalize: 1, rho_x: 1.00e-06
          acceleration_lookback: 50, acceleration_interval: 10
lin-sys:  sparse-direct-mkl-pardiso
          nnz(A): 275706, nnz(P): 0
```
the speed-up is comparable (`MKLDirectSolver` is 2-3 times faster here).

----
These might be atypical examples for showing off `MKLDirectSolver`: these problems are after symmetry reduction so there's a rather small number of small psd constraints with a bunch of **dense** linear constraints. 

The original version (with large psd constraint and lots of sparse linear constraints) of the first (small) problem is:
```
problem:  variables n: 93962, constraints m: 169158
cones:    z: primal zero / dual free vars: 75197
          s: psd vars: 93961, ssize: 1
settings: eps_abs: 1.0e-10, eps_rel: 1.0e-10, eps_infeas: 1.0e-07
          alpha: 1.90, scale: 1.00e-01, adaptive_scale: 1
          max_iters: 20000, normalize: 1, rho_x: 1.00e-06
          acceleration_lookback: 50, acceleration_interval: 10
lin-sys:  sparse-direct-amd-qdldl
          nnz(A): 746426, nnz(P): 0
```
`DirectSolver` runs in 
 * `1296` seconds (1 thread),
 * `1452` seconds (4 threads)
vs `MKLDirectSolver`
 * `1578` seconds (1 thread)
 * `1012` seconds (4 threads)

so `MKLDirectSolver` benefits from multiple threads (`OMP_NUM_THREADS`) while `DirectSolver` doesn't. Simply by looking at `htop` the `DirectSolver` seems to waste most of the resources (the occupied cores are predominantly in `sys: wait` state -- maybe some problem with synchronization/communication?). `MKLDirectSolver` fares much better here fully utilizing all available resources.

Tbh I hoped for something much better ;) But maybe those timings/findings are useful for @bodono as well.
